title: The Impact of Generative AI on Collaborative Open-Source Software Development: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
study_design_description: Using GitHub's proprietary Copilot usage data combined with public OSS project data, the study analyzes project-month-level outcomes from January 2021 to December 2022. The treatment group consists of projects where Copilot was supported by local coding environments and used by developers, while the control group includes projects where Copilot was not used throughout the sample period. The analysis employs the Generalized Synthetic Control Method (GSCM) to estimate treatment effects, with validation through different samples, matching techniques, instrumental variable estimation, and additional robustness checks.
primary_task_description: The study investigates how GitHub Copilot, an AI pair programmer, influences project-level code contributions and coordination time of open-source software projects on GitHub, as well as how its effect varies among core versus peripheral developers. In OSS settings, developers work asynchronously and coordinate without formal managerial structures. Core developers have write access and full control over projects, while peripheral developers' code must be reviewed by core developers for acceptance.
population_description: The sample includes OSS projects with non-zero size, at least one specified programming language and license, a description, and no mirror or personal store designation. Projects must have at least one code submission every six months from 2021 to 2022 and at least one additional activity each year. The analysis focuses on projects with at least three developers contributing each month. The final sample includes 7,637 projects, with 4,491 in the treatment group and 3,146 in the control group.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Copilot use increases project-level code contributions by 5.9%.
Copilot use also leads to an increase in coordination time by 8% due to more code discussions.
Copilot increased the number of developers whose PRs have been merged by 3.4%.
Copilot use is associated with a 2.1% increase in average number of merged PRs per developer in active projects.
Copilot adoption is associated with a 6.5% increase in code discussion volume per merged PR.
Copilot impacts code discussion participation, showing a significant 1.7% increase in code discussion participation.
Projects using Copilot show a 5.9% increase in code discussion intensity per developer.
Copilot adoption is associated with a 3.5% increase in PRs merged within one day, a 4.1% increase in PRs merged within three days, and a 5.1% increase in PRs merged within ten days.

Peripheral developers experienced an increase of 0.085 or 5.4% in their relative average merge time.

notes: This is an observational study using proprietary GitHub Copilot usage data combined with public GitHub project data. The primary estimation method is the Generalized Synthetic Control Method (GSCM), with extensive robustness checks including PSM+DID, instrumental variable analysis, and alternative specifications. The study finds heterogeneous effects between core and peripheral developers. Key limitations acknowledged by the authors include: (1) inability to observe individual-level Copilot usage due to privacy constraints, limiting measurement of heterogeneous adoption behaviors; (2) observational data cannot fully capture unobserved developer characteristics such as motivation, ability, or work preferences; (3) inability to identify intermediate mechanisms through which AI tools affect coordination; (4) short sample period (2021-2022) limits assessment of long-term effects; (5) potential spillover effects if developers use Copilot knowledge from other projects; (6) potential underestimation of effects if developers sign up gradually after initial availability; (7) the study does not examine broader social and governance implications. The paper reports percentage changes for all outcomes but does not provide exact p-values, confidence intervals, or standardized effect sizes in the quotes provided.
